texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: The best autoregressive approach to audio inpainting is gap-wise Janssen
texts.text: The best autoregressive approach to audio inpainting is gap-wise Janssen

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ondˇ rej Mokr´ y
texts.text: Ondˇ rej Mokr´ y

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Department of Telecommunications Brno University of Technology Czech Republic
texts.text: Department of Telecommunications Brno University of Technology Czech Republic

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract -A novel variant of the Janssen method for audio inpainting is presented. The new method is compared with a number of other popular audio inpainting methods based on autoregressive modeling. Main differences between the particular approaches are pointed out. In the experimental part, the importance of the choice of the AR model estimator is confirmed by objective metrics, and the effect of the chosen AR model order and window size is explored. The results of small-scale and midscale computational experiments are in agreement. The results show the superiority of the proposed gap-wise Janssen approach, which is confirmed by a listening test.
texts.text: Abstract -A novel variant of the Janssen method for audio inpainting is presented. The new method is compared with a number of other popular audio inpainting methods based on autoregressive modeling. Main differences between the particular approaches are pointed out. In the experimental part, the importance of the choice of the AR model estimator is confirmed by objective metrics, and the effect of the chosen AR model order and window size is explored. The results of small-scale and midscale computational experiments are in agreement. The results show the superiority of the proposed gap-wise Janssen approach, which is confirmed by a listening test.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Index Terms -audio, autoregression, inpainting, interpolation, comparison.
texts.text: Index Terms -audio, autoregression, inpainting, interpolation, comparison.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: I. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: I. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Audio inpainting is a challenging signal processing task, where missing parts of an audio signal have to be completed. For a human listener, the result should be as pleasant as possible and ideally free of artifacts. Previously proposed audio inpainting solutions cover a wide range of approaches, from autoregressive modeling [1], [2], [3], [4], through optimization methods [5], [6], [7], [8], [9], heuristics [10] to deep learning [11], [12], [13], [14] or graph-based methods [15].
texts.text: Audio inpainting is a challenging signal processing task, where missing parts of an audio signal have to be completed. For a human listener, the result should be as pleasant as possible and ideally free of artifacts. Previously proposed audio inpainting solutions cover a wide range of approaches, from autoregressive modeling [1], [2], [3], [4], through optimization methods [5], [6], [7], [8], [9], heuristics [10] to deep learning [11], [12], [13], [14] or graph-based methods [15].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For signal gaps of up to ca 80 milliseconds, the iterative method of Janssen et al. [1] proposed in 1986 constantly ranks among the best, according to numerous studies [6], [9], [16], [17]. The extrapolation methods [3], [18], [4], [19] are non-iterative and utilize a twofold extrapolation (from left to right and right to left) while the two particular solutions are blended together using a crossfading scheme. Such an approach belongs to the most popular, perhaps due to its simplicity and speed, and is actually used in the Matlab function fillgaps. The patented method of Etter [2] considers the just mentioned approach suboptimal and proposes to aggregate the two extrapolation directions in a single optimization criterion.
texts.text: For signal gaps of up to ca 80 milliseconds, the iterative method of Janssen et al. [1] proposed in 1986 constantly ranks among the best, according to numerous studies [6], [9], [16], [17]. The extrapolation methods [3], [18], [4], [19] are non-iterative and utilize a twofold extrapolation (from left to right and right to left) while the two particular solutions are blended together using a crossfading scheme. Such an approach belongs to the most popular, perhaps due to its simplicity and speed, and is actually used in the Matlab function fillgaps. The patented method of Etter [2] considers the just mentioned approach suboptimal and proposes to aggregate the two extrapolation directions in a single optimization criterion.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Besides slight variances in how to model the signal , different algorithms for estimation of the coefficients are also available [20], which will be discussed in detail further on.
texts.text: Besides slight variances in how to model the signal , different algorithms for estimation of the coefficients are also available [20], which will be discussed in detail further on.

Most importantly, we propose a new variant of the Janssen algorithm [1] not present in the literature and examine its performance compared with known approaches. This novel method does not rely on frame-wise signal processing, in contrast to the original method.
texts.text: particular popular approaches, and present computational experiments on two audio datasets. Most importantly, we propose a new variant of the Janssen algorithm [1] not present in the literature and examine its performance compared with known approaches. This novel method does not rely on frame-wise signal processing, in contrast to the original method.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper considers only gaps up to 80 ms in length. For larger gap sizes, autoregression usually starts to become inefficient. A number of non-autoregressive methods cited above were designed to cope with larger gaps; however, note that it is challenging to compare against such methods for at least two reasons: first, they are typically trained on a specific class of data, while AR modeling is data-independent; second, these methods fill the gaps with material which may be pleasant to listen to, but it does not have to align with the original audio, making it hard to objectively judge the reconstruction quality.
texts.text: This paper considers only gaps up to 80 ms in length. For larger gap sizes, autoregression usually starts to become inefficient. A number of non-autoregressive methods cited above were designed to cope with larger gaps; however, note that it is challenging to compare against such methods for at least two reasons: first, they are typically trained on a specific class of data, while AR modeling is data-independent; second, these methods fill the gaps with material which may be pleasant to listen to, but it does not have to align with the original audio, making it hard to objectively judge the reconstruction quality.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: II. MODELING AUDIO AS AN AUTOREGRESSIVE PROCESS
texts.text: II. MODELING AUDIO AS AN AUTOREGRESSIVE PROCESS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A signal x = [ x 1 , . . . , x N ] /latticetop ∈ R N is said to be modeled as an autoregressive (AR) process, if it satisfies
texts.text: A signal x = [ x 1 , . . . , x N ] /latticetop ∈ R N is said to be modeled as an autoregressive (AR) process, if it satisfies

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where e = [ e 1 , . . . , e N + p ] /latticetop is a realization of a zero-mean white noise process, and a = [1 , a 2 , . . . , a p +1 ] /latticetop are referred to as the AR coefficients [20, Def. 3.1.2]. The order p defines the range of indexes that determine the output in the current time instance. As such, the frequency resolution increases with increasing p . The above is closely related to the notion of convolution, and it is actually in accordance with the convention of the lpc function in Matlab.
texts.text: where e = [ e 1 , . . . , e N + p ] /latticetop is a realization of a zero-mean white noise process, and a = [1 , a 2 , . . . , a p +1 ] /latticetop are referred to as the AR coefficients [20, Def. 3.1.2]. The order p defines the range of indexes that determine the output in the current time instance. As such, the frequency resolution increases with increasing p . The above is closely related to the notion of convolution, and it is actually in accordance with the convention of the lpc function in Matlab.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From the perspective of model fitting, the vector e ∈ R N + p is called the residual error [21, Sec. 8.2.2]. Given the observed signal x and the order p , the coefficients of the AR model are usually estimated via the optimization problem
texts.text: From the perspective of model fitting, the vector e ∈ R N + p is called the residual error [21, Sec. 8.2.2]. Given the observed signal x and the order p , the coefficients of the AR model are usually estimated via the optimization problem

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: arg min a 1 2 ‖ e ( a , x ) ‖ 2 , (2)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where the error e ( a , x ) , defined in (1), is a function of both the signal x and the coefficients a . Problem (2) can be effectively solved using an estimate of the autocorrelation and the Levinson-Durbin algorithm [22], [23]. Such an approach is typically referred to as the LPC for its connection to the linear prediction coefficients.
texts.text: where the error e ( a , x ) , defined in (1), is a function of both the signal x and the coefficients a . Problem (2) can be effectively solved using an estimate of the autocorrelation and the Levinson-Durbin algorithm [22], [23]. Such an approach is typically referred to as the LPC for its connection to the linear prediction coefficients.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In contrast to LPC, the Burg algorithm [24] for the estimation of the AR parameters involves an extra assumption that the same parameters should model both the signal x and its version flipped in time. In effect, this results in another quadratic term extending (2). Kauppinen and Roth prefer the Burg algorithm for audio signal extrapolation [18, Sec. 4.2] since the underlying all-pole filter obtained this way is stable [25, Sec. 12.3.3]. A frequency-warped Burg algorithm has been proposed [19] that allows focusing on specified spectral bands; however, the effect of warping is comparable to increasing the model order p in the non-warped case [19, Sec. 5].
texts.text: In contrast to LPC, the Burg algorithm [24] for the estimation of the AR parameters involves an extra assumption that the same parameters should model both the signal x and its version flipped in time. In effect, this results in another quadratic term extending (2). Kauppinen and Roth prefer the Burg algorithm for audio signal extrapolation [18, Sec. 4.2] since the underlying all-pole filter obtained this way is stable [25, Sec. 12.3.3]. A frequency-warped Burg algorithm has been proposed [19] that allows focusing on specified spectral bands; however, the effect of warping is comparable to increasing the model order p in the non-warped case [19, Sec. 5].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Additional terms can be optionally appended to the objective (2) that regularize either the reconstructed signal or the AR coefficients, as proposed in [26], [27].
texts.text: Additional terms can be optionally appended to the objective (2) that regularize either the reconstructed signal or the AR coefficients, as proposed in [26], [27].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: III. AUDIO INPAINTING USING AUTOREGRESSION
texts.text: III. AUDIO INPAINTING USING AUTOREGRESSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To formalize the problem of audio inpainting, assume that the observed signal x ∈ R N consists of reliable samples identified by the set of indices M ⊂ { 1 , . . . , N } , and vacant samples at positions M = { 1 , . . . , N } \ M . The goal of inpainting is to estimate the missing samples at the positions M . In the so-called consistent case, the samples at positions M are meant to be preserved, i.e., any candidate solution ˆ x to the inpainting problem should satisfy ˆ x n = x n for all n ∈ M .
texts.text: To formalize the problem of audio inpainting, assume that the observed signal x ∈ R N consists of reliable samples identified by the set of indices M ⊂ { 1 , . . . , N } , and vacant samples at positions M = { 1 , . . . , N } \ M . The goal of inpainting is to estimate the missing samples at the positions M . In the so-called consistent case, the samples at positions M are meant to be preserved, i.e., any candidate solution ˆ x to the inpainting problem should satisfy ˆ x n = x n for all n ∈ M .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, the focus is on the practical scenario where the signal contains gaps, i.e., segments of consecutive lost samples, surrounded by an intact context. For this scenario, two approaches based on AR modeling are applicable.
texts.text: In this work, the focus is on the practical scenario where the signal contains gaps, i.e., segments of consecutive lost samples, surrounded by an intact context. For this scenario, two approaches based on AR modeling are applicable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First, the extrapolation-based method fits two independent sets of AR parameters for each gap, one for the left-hand context and one for the right-hand context of the gap. These coefficients are then used to extrapolate (predict) both contexts inside the gap, and the forward- and backward-extrapolated signals are then cross-faded. Numerous fading options are possible (see, for instance, [3, Sec. 4.2]); we resort to the raised cosine function used in [2].
texts.text: First, the extrapolation-based method fits two independent sets of AR parameters for each gap, one for the left-hand context and one for the right-hand context of the gap. These coefficients are then used to extrapolate (predict) both contexts inside the gap, and the forward- and backward-extrapolated signals are then cross-faded. Numerous fading options are possible (see, for instance, [3, Sec. 4.2]); we resort to the raised cosine function used in [2].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Second, the Janssen method is based upon alternating estimation of the AR model for a signal frame and the missing samples in this frame [1]. The method iteratively updates the AR coefficients of a signal frame (the current estimate of the missing samples being fixed) and the missing samples (with a fixed estimate of the model parameters). As such, it solves a problem similar to (2), where both a and x are variables, and x is constrained to stick to the reliable part of a signal frame. Since the AR model is most suitable for stationary signals, the procedure is usually applied independently in (overlapping) temporal signal frames.
texts.text: Second, the Janssen method is based upon alternating estimation of the AR model for a signal frame and the missing samples in this frame [1]. The method iteratively updates the AR coefficients of a signal frame (the current estimate of the missing samples being fixed) and the missing samples (with a fixed estimate of the model parameters). As such, it solves a problem similar to (2), where both a and x are variables, and x is constrained to stick to the reliable part of a signal frame. Since the AR model is most suitable for stationary signals, the procedure is usually applied independently in (overlapping) temporal signal frames.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Finally, we propose a novel use of the Janssen method, which, instead of overlapping frames, treats each gap in the signal separately (hence the name gap-wise Janssen). Here, a single AR model is simultaneously fitted to both left- and right-hand contexts of the gap. 1 Such a way of using the context is similar to the extrapolation-based method, but the AR coefficients (shared by both contexts) are estimated as in the Janssen method. Apparently, the proposed approach cannot be used in scenarios with random positions of missing samples, in contrast to the window-based version.
texts.text: Finally, we propose a novel use of the Janssen method, which, instead of overlapping frames, treats each gap in the signal separately (hence the name gap-wise Janssen). Here, a single AR model is simultaneously fitted to both left- and right-hand contexts of the gap. 1 Such a way of using the context is similar to the extrapolation-based method, but the AR coefficients (shared by both contexts) are estimated as in the Janssen method. Apparently, the proposed approach cannot be used in scenarios with random positions of missing samples, in contrast to the window-based version.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: IV. EXPERIMENTS & RESULTS
texts.text: IV. EXPERIMENTS & RESULTS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The first dataset consists of 9 recordings of individual musical instruments 2 , taken from the EBU SQAM database [28]. They are sampled at 44.1 kHz and cut to a length of around 7 seconds.
texts.text: The first dataset consists of 9 recordings of individual musical instruments 2 , taken from the EBU SQAM database [28]. They are sampled at 44.1 kHz and cut to a length of around 7 seconds.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To simulate degradation, we consider gap lengths from 10 ms up to 80 ms, and create 10 gaps in each signal at pseudorandom locations. 3 From AR-based methods, we use all three aforementioned approaches: The extrapolation-based method and the gap-wise Janssen method are applied with a fixed context length of 4096 samples (approx. 93 ms) on each side of the gap (i.e., 8192 samples in total). The framewise Janssen uses a window length of 4096 samples and two window shapes: rectangular and Hann (see, e.g., [29, Sec. V]). All methods are applied with the varying model order p and either the Burg or the LPC algorithm to fit the AR model.
texts.text: To simulate degradation, we consider gap lengths from 10 ms up to 80 ms, and create 10 gaps in each signal at pseudorandom locations. 3 From AR-based methods, we use all three aforementioned approaches: The extrapolation-based method and the gap-wise Janssen method are applied with a fixed context length of 4096 samples (approx. 93 ms) on each side of the gap (i.e., 8192 samples in total). The framewise Janssen uses a window length of 4096 samples and two window shapes: rectangular and Hann (see, e.g., [29, Sec. V]). All methods are applied with the varying model order p and either the Burg or the LPC algorithm to fit the AR model.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The first measure of reconstruction quality is the signalto-distortion ratio (SDR). For the reference (i.e., undegraded) signal y and the reconstruction ˆ x , SDR in decibels is computed as SDR ( y , ˆ x ) = 10log 10 ‖ y ‖ 2 ‖ y -ˆ x ‖ 2 . In our case, the SDR is only computed in the inpainted sections of the signal. The perceived quality of the signal is assessed using PEMO-Q [30], an objective metric which predicts the subjective difference of y and ˆ x in terms of the objective difference grade (ODG), ranging from -4 (very annoying) to 0 (imperceptible). An alternative choice, which is common in other audio processing fields, is PEAQ [31], [32]. However, this metric is not decisive enough in the case of gap inpainting [33].
texts.text: The first measure of reconstruction quality is the signalto-distortion ratio (SDR). For the reference (i.e., undegraded) signal y and the reconstruction ˆ x , SDR in decibels is computed as SDR ( y , ˆ x ) = 10log 10 ‖ y ‖ 2 ‖ y -ˆ x ‖ 2 . In our case, the SDR is only computed in the inpainted sections of the signal. The perceived quality of the signal is assessed using PEMO-Q [30], an objective metric which predicts the subjective difference of y and ˆ x in terms of the objective difference grade (ODG), ranging from -4 (very annoying) to 0 (imperceptible). An alternative choice, which is common in other audio processing fields, is PEAQ [31], [32]. However, this metric is not decisive enough in the case of gap inpainting [33].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note that further experiments were performed using a longer window/context length of 8192 samples, and also using a midscale dataset based upon the IRMAS database [34], [35]; see Sec. IV-D.
texts.text: Note that further experiments were performed using a longer window/context length of 8192 samples, and also using a midscale dataset based upon the IRMAS database [34], [35]; see Sec. IV-D.

texts.orig: A. Effect of the estimator
texts.text: A. Effect of the estimator

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First, we evaluate the performance of the inpainting methods depending on the estimator of the AR model parameters. Hence, for each test instance, two versions of each inpainting method are run, using either the LPC or the Burg algorithm.
texts.text: First, we evaluate the performance of the inpainting methods depending on the estimator of the AR model parameters. Hence, for each test instance, two versions of each inpainting method are run, using either the LPC or the Burg algorithm.

The overall distribution of the results in the scatter plots indicates that, in terms of the ODG, the Burg algorithm is clearly favorable in the case of the extrapolation-based inpainting. The preferability of the Burg algorithm is also indicated in the gap-wise Janssen

texts.label: text
texts.orig: algorithm. Notably, a conclusion in the case of the framewise Janssen algorithm depends on the model order and the selected window. With the Hann window, inpainting results appear to depend on the chosen estimator only for large model orders (LPC scores better in such cases). With the rectangular window, the dependence on the model order is clearly amplified. Furthermore, a large portion of the results in this case implies that the Burg algorithm is a better choice if the model order is low, and vice versa. The differences have been verified as statistically significant using the Wilcoxon signed rank test [36].
texts.text: algorithm. Notably, a conclusion in the case of the framewise Janssen algorithm depends on the model order and the selected window. With the Hann window, inpainting results appear to depend on the chosen estimator only for large model orders (LPC scores better in such cases). With the rectangular window, the dependence on the model order is clearly amplified. Furthermore, a large portion of the results in this case implies that the Burg algorithm is a better choice if the model order is low, and vice versa. The differences have been verified as statistically significant using the Wilcoxon signed rank test [36].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: B. Effect of the model order
texts.text: B. Effect of the model order

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The AR model order p plays a crucial role in the modeling and also significantly affects the results, as demonstrated in Fig. 2. Note that Fig. 2 plots the same data as Fig. 1, but with the focus on the effect of the model order and the gap length.
texts.text: The AR model order p plays a crucial role in the modeling and also significantly affects the results, as demonstrated in Fig. 2. Note that Fig. 2 plots the same data as Fig. 1, but with the focus on the effect of the model order and the gap length.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Similarly to the study of preferences between LPC and the Burg algorithm, the model order affects the results differently for different inpainting methods. A clear scheme is observed in the case of the extrapolation-based and the gap-wise Janssen methods, where increasing the model order up to p = 2048 results in both a higher SDR and a higher ODG. However, the
texts.text: Similarly to the study of preferences between LPC and the Burg algorithm, the model order affects the results differently for different inpainting methods. A clear scheme is observed in the case of the extrapolation-based and the gap-wise Janssen methods, where increasing the model order up to p = 2048 results in both a higher SDR and a higher ODG. However, the

texts.label: text
texts.orig: order p = 3072 does not further increase the resulting quality. Note that the same observation holds for the experiment with window/context length 8192, including model order p = 4096 ; see the supplementary material online. For the frame-wise Janssen, the results are further affected by the chosen window shape: the best results are achieved using p = 512 with the rectangular window, and p = 1024 with the Hann window.
texts.text: order p = 3072 does not further increase the resulting quality. Note that the same observation holds for the experiment with window/context length 8192, including model order p = 4096 ; see the supplementary material online. For the frame-wise Janssen, the results are further affected by the chosen window shape: the best results are achieved using p = 512 with the rectangular window, and p = 1024 with the Hann window.

texts.orig: Furthermore, Fig. 2 reveals that the observed phenomena do not depend on the length of the gap.
texts.text: Furthermore, Fig. 2 reveals that the observed phenomena do not depend on the length of the gap.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: C. Comparison with other methods
texts.text: C. Comparison with other methods

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To provide a context for the results of AR-based methods, selected variants are compared with the methods that belong to the state-of-the-art in optimization-based audio inpainting, namely A-SPAIN [6] and A-SPAIN-MOD [16], applied with the same window length 4096 samples. The results in terms of SDR and ODG are presented in Fig. 3. The most significant observation is the dominance of the extrapolation-based and, especially, the proposed gap-wise Janssen methods, in particular in gaps larger than 50 ms.
texts.text: To provide a context for the results of AR-based methods, selected variants are compared with the methods that belong to the state-of-the-art in optimization-based audio inpainting, namely A-SPAIN [6] and A-SPAIN-MOD [16], applied with the same window length 4096 samples. The results in terms of SDR and ODG are presented in Fig. 3. The most significant observation is the dominance of the extrapolation-based and, especially, the proposed gap-wise Janssen methods, in particular in gaps larger than 50 ms.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to objective metrics, a subjective listening test was performed on a subset of three audio excerpts (the violin, piano, and clarinet) with gaps of 20, 50 and 80 milliseconds in length, making 9 test signals altogether. Such a combination of signals, gap lengths and reconstruction methods yields a total length of 8.5 minutes of netto audio to evaluate. We ran a MUSHRA-type test [37], using the webMUSHRA environment [38], in which the participants evaluated the quality of several reconstructions. The test was run in a quiet

texts.orig: selection contains a wide range of music characters and genres but avoids recordings with highly pronounced drums. Each final excerpt is 7 seconds long. The test material is much more complex than in the solo-instrument case, thus weakening the assumption about the autoregressive nature of signals, which explains reaching lower values of both SDR and ODG and all the methods considered. However, the results exhibit the same behavior as the small-scale experiment from Fig. 3; in particular, the ranking of the methods was identical.

texts.orig: Finally, all the experiments were repeated using a longer window/context length of 8192 samples. This change is beneficial especially for the windowed methods, see Fig. 5; however, the gap-wise Janssen remains superior on average. For complete results, see the accompanying webpage.
texts.text: Finally, all the experiments were repeated using a longer window/context length of 8192 samples. This change is beneficial especially for the windowed methods, see Fig. 5; however, the gap-wise Janssen remains superior on average. For complete results, see the accompanying webpage.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: V. CONCLUSION
texts.text: V. CONCLUSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A novel gap-wise modification of the Janssen method was proposed and evaluated against popular audio inpainting methods based on autoregressive modeling. The experiments demonstrated the importance of the choice of the AR model estimator (i.e., choosing the LPC or the Burg algorithm) and the model order. The concluding tests, both objective and subjective, revealed that the gap-wise Janssen method (using the Burg algorithm) is recommended as an autoregressive reference for inpainting of gaps up to 80 ms; this holds even in comparison with sparsity-based approaches.
texts.text: A novel gap-wise modification of the Janssen method was proposed and evaluated against popular audio inpainting methods based on autoregressive modeling. The experiments demonstrated the importance of the choice of the AR model estimator (i.e., choosing the LPC or the Burg algorithm) and the model order. The concluding tests, both objective and subjective, revealed that the gap-wise Janssen method (using the Burg algorithm) is recommended as an autoregressive reference for inpainting of gaps up to 80 ms; this holds even in comparison with sparsity-based approaches.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If computational speed is an important criterion, note that for all approaches, the computational load is proportional both to the order of the AR model and to the gap length. Moreover, the Burg algorithm is more demanding compared to the LPC. From the perspective of computational time, the extrapolationbased approach is clearly preferable (elapsed times are up to around 0.15 s per signal with p = 2048 , while the gap-wise Janssen reaches up to 11.5 s per signal with p = 1024 , and up to 16 s with p = 2048 ; both using the Burg algorithm).
texts.text: If computational speed is an important criterion, note that for all approaches, the computational load is proportional both to the order of the AR model and to the gap length. Moreover, the Burg algorithm is more demanding compared to the LPC. From the perspective of computational time, the extrapolationbased approach is clearly preferable (elapsed times are up to around 0.15 s per signal with p = 2048 , while the gap-wise Janssen reaches up to 11.5 s per signal with p = 1024 , and up to 16 s with p = 2048 ; both using the Burg algorithm).
